section.title: Warning to the Rich
section.sectionRationale: James returns a final time to wealth, now not to warn the church against favoring the rich but to thunder against oppressive rich people themselves. In the voice of an Old Testament prophet he pronounces coming misery on those who hoard, defraud their workers, live in luxury, and condemn the righteous. Their rotting riches and unpaid wages testify against them before the Lord of Hosts.
section.thesis: Wealth hoarded and gained by fraud and injustice will rot and testify against its owners in the last days, for the cries of the defrauded reach the Lord of Hosts, who will judge the self-indulgent oppressors of the righteous.
section.frameNote: Two movements: corroded riches as a witness in the last days; and the withheld wages, luxury, and bloodshed that cry out to the Lord of Hosts.

units.label: Rotting riches, a witness against you
units.note: treasure hoarded in the last days

verses.text: Come now, you who are rich, weep and wail over the misery to come upon you.
Your riches have rotted and moths have eaten your clothes.
Your gold and silver are corroded. Their corrosion will testify against you and consume your flesh like fire. You have {{thesaurizo|hoarded treasure}} in the last days.

units.label: The cries that reach the Lord of Hosts

verses.text: Look, the wages you withheld from the workmen who mowed your fields are crying out against you. The cries of the harvesters have reached the ears of the {{sabaoth|Lord of Hosts}}.
You have lived on earth in {{truphao|luxury}} and self-indulgence. You have fattened your hearts in the day of slaughter.
You have condemned and murdered the righteous, who did not resist you.

thesaurizo.gloss: To store up, hoard, lay up treasure. James charges the rich with hoarding wealth in the last days, the very time to be generous and ready for the Lord's coming. The word echoes Jesus' warning against laying up treasure on earth rather than treasure in heaven.

sabaoth.gloss: Hosts, armies; the Lord of Hosts, or Lord of Sabaoth, is the Lord who commands the armies of heaven. This transliterated Hebrew title portrays God as the all-powerful, sovereign King who hears the cries of the oppressed and marches to their defense. The defrauded laborers have this mighty Judge on their side.

truphao.gloss: To live in luxury, indulgence, and soft self-pleasing. James condemns the rich for a pampered life built on the suffering of others, fattening themselves like animals unaware of the day of slaughter. Such self-indulgence, feeding on injustice, ripens them for judgment.

basic.context: James suddenly sounds like an Old Testament prophet, thundering at rich oppressors. Weep and wail, he says, because misery is coming. Your hoarded wealth is already rotting, your fine clothes moth-eaten, your gold corroded, and that decay will be a witness against you. Worse, you cheated your workers, keeping back their pay, and those unpaid wages are crying out to God, the Lord of armies, who hears. You lived in luxury while others suffered, fattening yourselves for the day of slaughter, and you even condemned and killed the innocent, who did not fight back. It is a warning of certain judgment on wealth gained and used unjustly.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic denunciation, and it is important to see exactly what James condemns. He does not condemn wealth as such, but wealth hoarded, gained by fraud, spent on self-indulgence, and used to oppress the righteous. The rich addressed may include unbelieving oppressors outside the church, so this functions partly as comfort to the suffering poor: God sees, and He will judge. The last days language shows this is not merely ancient history but the present age before Christ's return.
basic.originalLanguages: The rich thesaurizo, hoard treasure, in the last days. Their victims' cries reach the sabaoth, the Lord of Hosts, the God of armies. And they live in truphao, luxury and self-indulgence, fattening themselves for slaughter. The words move from hoarding to the cry of the oppressed to a doomed life of ease.
basic.typology: The prophetic woe against oppressive wealth echoes Amos and Isaiah and points forward to the final judgment of Babylon the great in Revelation, where the merchants' riches come to nothing. The righteous who did not resist, condemned and murdered, foreshadows Christ, the ultimate righteous One who did not resist those who condemned Him.
basic.christ: The righteous one who is condemned and murdered yet does not resist points powerfully to Jesus, who like a lamb was led to slaughter and opened not His mouth. The Lord of Hosts who hears the cries of the oppressed is the God who vindicates His Son and all who suffer in Him. And Jesus' own warnings against hoarding treasure and His blessing on the poor stand behind this passage.
basic.god: God is the Lord of Hosts, the almighty King who hears the cries of the defrauded and the oppressed and will surely act. He is the righteous Judge before whom hoarded wealth becomes evidence and unpaid wages become an accusation. He is not indifferent to injustice; He sees the luxury of the powerful and the suffering of the weak, and He will set the account right.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus warned against laying up treasure on earth, Matthew 6:19-21, and about the danger of riches, Luke 6:24. The withheld wages recall Deuteronomy 24:14-15 and Malachi 3:5. The Lord of Hosts hearing the oppressed echoes Isaiah 5:9. And Revelation 18 depicts the fall of luxurious Babylon.
basic.oneStory: The prophets thundered against the rich who trampled the poor, and God always heard the cry of the oppressed, from Israel groaning in Egypt to the harvesters cheated of their wages. James takes up that prophetic voice in the last days, warning that hoarded, unjust wealth will testify against its owners before the Lord of Hosts. The righteous who suffer without resisting share the path of Christ, the true righteous One, and await the day when the Judge who hears every cry will make all things right.

context.literary: The paragraph opens with come now, echoing the address to the presumptuous planners in chapter 4, but the tone sharpens into prophetic woe. It divides into the indictment of decaying, hoarded riches, verses 1 to 3, and the catalog of sins that cry to God, verses 4 to 6. It stands in deliberate contrast to the patient sufferers addressed immediately afterward.
context.historical: James writes in a world of stark inequality, where wealthy landowners could withhold the daily wages on which hired harvesters depended for survival, and where the powerful could manipulate courts against the poor. Some of the rich in view are likely the oppressors outside the church who dragged believers into court, as chapter 2 noted, though the warning applies to any who use wealth this way.
context.canonical: The passage stands squarely in the prophetic tradition of Amos, Isaiah, and Micah, which denounced luxury built on the backs of the poor and promised divine judgment. Its title Lord of Hosts, its image of crying wages, and its last days horizon tie it to both the Old Testament prophets and the New Testament expectation of Christ's return and final judgment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To pronounce prophetic judgment on the rich who hoard wealth, defraud their workers, live in self-indulgent luxury, and oppress the righteous, and thereby to assure the suffering poor that the Lord of Hosts hears their cries and will judge.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is more denunciation than direct command, describing the sins of the oppressive rich and their coming judgment. Its implicit prescription is a solemn warning: do not hoard, defraud, or indulge at others' expense, and, for the oppressed, take comfort that God will act. It calls all readers to hold wealth justly and generously in light of judgment.

debates.question: Does James condemn wealth itself, or its misuse?

views.label: The misuse of wealth, not wealth as such
views.case: James targets specific sins: hoarding, fraud against workers, self-indulgent luxury, and oppression of the righteous. Scripture elsewhere includes godly wealthy believers, and James earlier honored the poor without demonizing all riches. The woe falls on unjust acquisition and use, not on possessing money.

views.label: A sweeping prophetic woe on the rich as a class
views.case: Others note how absolute the language is, addressed simply to you who are rich, in the tradition of prophetic woes and Jesus' woe to the rich in Luke 6:24. On this reading it is a stark warning about the spiritual peril of riches in general.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage sounds a grave warning about wealth. The consistent biblical witness is that riches are perilous and must be held justly and generously, or they become a witness against their owner.

debates.question: Who are the rich being addressed?

views.label: Oppressors outside the church
views.case: The rich here defraud workers, live in luxury, and murder the righteous, matching the wealthy oppressors of chapter 2 who dragged believers to court. On this view James addresses them prophetically while comforting the oppressed believers who overhear.

views.label: A warning also to any wealthy within the church
views.case: Others hold the warning reaches wealthy members too, pressing every rich believer to examine whether their wealth is hoarded or unjustly used. The last days horizon makes it a live warning for all.

debates.bearing: Whether aimed chiefly at outside oppressors or at any rich hearer, the effect is the same: God will judge unjust wealth, and the oppressed can trust that He hears.

study.originalLanguages: James wields prophetic language with precision. The rich thesaurizo, hoard treasure, and do so in the last days, the very season for generosity before the Lord's coming, so their storing up is a storing up of wrath. The unpaid wages of the harvesters cry out and reach the ears of the sabaoth, the Lord of Hosts, the transliterated Hebrew title for the God who commands heaven's armies and marches to defend the oppressed. Meanwhile the rich truphao, live in luxury and soft self-indulgence, fattening their hearts like unwitting cattle in the day of slaughter. The vocabulary itself frames wealth misused as evidence, accusation, and doom before an almighty Judge.

typology.type: The righteous condemned and murdered, not resisting
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows Christ, the righteous One led like a lamb to slaughter who opened not His mouth, Isaiah 53:7, Acts 8:32.

typology.type: The Lord of Hosts hearing the cries of the oppressed
typology.fulfillment: The God who heard Israel in Egypt and vindicates His people, fully in the just Judge who returns in Christ.
Anticipates the fall of Babylon the great, whose riches come to nothing, Revelation 18.

study.christ: The closing line, you have condemned and murdered the righteous, who did not resist you, points beyond any single victim to Christ, the truly Righteous One, unjustly condemned and killed, who like a lamb before its shearers did not open His mouth. The Lord of Hosts who hears the harvesters' cries is the God who vindicated His Son by raising Him, and who will vindicate all who suffer in Him. Jesus' own warnings against hoarding earthly treasure and His woe upon the self-satisfied rich stand behind every line of this passage.
study.god: God is the Lord of Hosts, the almighty commander of heaven's armies, who bends His ear to the cries of cheated laborers and oppressed believers. He is the righteous Judge before whom hoarded riches corrode into evidence and withheld wages become a shout of accusation. He is not blind to the luxury of the powerful or deaf to the suffering of the weak; He sees, He hears, and in the last days He will render a just account, setting right every wrong the powerful thought they had buried.

crossReferences.note: Do not lay up treasure on earth where moth and rust destroy, directly behind rotting riches and hoarded treasure.
Woe to you who are rich and full now, Jesus' prophetic woe paralleling James.
Do not withhold a hired worker's wages; his cry would reach the LORD, behind verse 4.
Woe to those who amass houses and fields; the Lord of Hosts has sworn judgment.
The fall of Babylon the great, whose luxurious wealth is suddenly ruined, echoing James's warning.

study.oneStory: The prophets thundered against the wealthy who trampled the poor, and God always heard the cry of the oppressed, from Israel's groaning in Egypt to the wages of cheated harvesters. James takes up that ancient prophetic voice in the last days, declaring that hoarded, ill-gotten riches will testify against their owners before the Lord of Hosts. The righteous who suffer without resisting walk the road of Christ, the true Righteous One condemned and killed, and they await the day when the Judge who hears every cry returns to make all things right.

quiz.q: What does James say will happen to the hoarded riches of the wicked?

options.text: They rot and corrode, becoming a witness that testifies against their owners.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3: the decayed wealth itself condemns them.

options.text: Their gold and silver corrode and will consume their flesh like fire.
options.note: True to verse 3, vivid imagery of the same judgment.

options.text: They will be safely preserved for future generations.
options.note: The opposite: the riches rot and testify against them.

options.text: They will guarantee the owners' security before God.
options.note: Hoarded wealth becomes evidence against them, not security.

quiz.q: What sin against workers does James highlight in verse 4?

options.text: Withholding the wages of the harvesters, whose cries reach the Lord of Hosts.
options.note: Verse 4: defrauded wages cry out to the almighty Judge.

options.text: Cheating laborers of pay they depended on to live.
options.note: True to the sense, naming the injustice involved.

options.text: Paying workers slightly late on occasion.
options.note: The charge is withholding wages outright, a grave injustice.

options.text: Giving workers too much rest.
options.note: The sin is exploitation, not generosity toward workers.

quiz.q: Does this passage condemn all wealth or its misuse?

options.text: It condemns hoarding, fraud, luxury, and oppression, the misuse of wealth.
options.note: The specific sins named are unjust acquisition and use, not mere possession.

options.text: It sounds a sweeping prophetic woe on the peril of riches.
options.note: True to the stark tone, echoing Jesus' woe to the rich.

options.text: It teaches that money is entirely neutral and never dangerous.
options.note: Scripture and James both warn of the real peril of riches.

options.text: It commands believers to become wealthy.
options.note: The passage warns the rich, it does not commend pursuing riches.

quiz.q: Who is the Lord of Hosts in verse 4?

options.text: The almighty God, commander of heaven's armies, who hears the oppressed.
options.note: The title portrays God as sovereign King and defender of the wronged.

options.text: The mighty Judge on the side of the defrauded laborers.
options.note: True to the comfort in the title, if less full than its meaning.

options.text: A minor angelic figure with limited power.
options.note: The title names God Himself as Lord of the heavenly armies.

options.text: An impersonal force indifferent to human affairs.
options.note: He personally hears and will act on the cries of the oppressed.

quiz.q: How does the description of the righteous in verse 6 point beyond itself?

options.text: The righteous condemned and murdered, not resisting, foreshadows Christ.
options.note: It echoes the Righteous One led like a lamb to slaughter, Isaiah 53.

options.text: It pictures innocent sufferers who do not fight back against injustice.
options.note: True to the immediate sense, the pattern fulfilled in Jesus.

options.text: It shows that the righteous always triumph immediately in this life.
options.note: Here the righteous suffer now; vindication comes from the Judge.

options.text: It teaches that the righteous deserve their suffering.
options.note: They are innocent, oppressed by the unjust rich.

questions: James warns against hoarding wealth in the last days. Where might you be storing up for yourself rather than being generous and ready for the Lord's coming?
God hears the cries of cheated workers. Are there people whose labor you benefit from who are treated unjustly, and how might you respond?
The rich fattened their hearts in luxury while others suffered. How can comfort and plenty dull us to the needs of the poor around us?
This passage assures the oppressed that the Lord of Hosts hears them. Where do you need to trust that God sees an injustice done to you or to others?
The righteous who suffered without resisting point to Christ. How does His unjust suffering and vindication shape the way you bear wrongs done to you?

translationNotes.note: Hoarded treasure renders thesaurizo, to store up; the BSB prints a line break within the verse before You have hoarded treasure, which is set here as running prose.
Lord of Hosts renders Lord Sabaoth, a transliterated Hebrew title for the Lord of the heavenly armies, stressing God's power to defend the oppressed.
Luxury renders truphao, to live in soft self-indulgence; fattened your hearts in the day of slaughter pictures unwitting animals being readied for judgment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for James 5:1-6. A line break within verse 3 is set as running prose. No em or en dashes occur in this movement.
